region_of_origin: Middle East / Levant; widely adopted in Turkish cuisine
food_subgroup: Pastry-based desserts with cheese filling and sugar syrup

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for kunafa/künefe. FOODON_03311069 (pastry food product) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Pastries and cakes) is the closest parent. No specific kunafa entry exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for kunafa or künefe as a specific food entity.

main_ingredients: unsalted white cheese (typically akkawi or mozzarella)
rose water or orange blossom water

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of kunafa is approximately 150 g, representing one individual portion of the baked cheese pastry with syrup.

nutrient_source.title: Knafeh (Kunafa) – composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entries for kataifi/shredded wheat pastry, whole-milk mozzarella cheese, butter, and sugar syrup
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service – FoodData Central (Foundation Foods and SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for kunafa as a prepared dish. Values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for the primary components: shredded wheat pastry (kataifi dough, ~40% by weight), whole-milk mozzarella cheese (~25%), clarified butter (~10%), and sugar syrup (~25%). Proportions are based on standard recipe references. Energy and macros are cross-checked against published Middle Eastern dessert composition data in peer-reviewed food composition literature.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sweet pastry and syrup-soaked desserts – analogous estimate based on baklava and syrup-soaked wheat pastry GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for kunafa/künefe was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated by analogy with syrup-soaked wheat-based pastry desserts (e.g., baklava GI ~55–70, sweet pastry with sugar syrup GI ~60–70) documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI literature. A midpoint of 65 (medium GI) is used. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (65 × 43.0 × 1.50) / 100 = 42.1 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~210 mg/100 g) from cheese and butter, combined with high energy density
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit kunafa to occasional small portions, as the sodium from akkawi or mozzarella cheese and the high caloric density may contribute to blood pressure elevation with regular consumption.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate and sugar content (~43 g carbohydrate, ~22 g sugar per 100 g) with medium GI of 65 producing a high glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat kunafa as an occasional indulgence only; the high sugar syrup content and medium GI result in a high glycemic load (~42 per serving) that can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation, and portion size should be strictly limited.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~7.8 g/100 g) from butter and full-fat cheese, and high sugar content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should consume kunafa sparingly, as the saturated fat from clarified butter and full-fat cheese, combined with the high sugar load, may adversely affect LDL cholesterol and triglyceride levels with frequent intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kunafa (also spelled knafeh, kanafeh, or künefe in Turkish) is a traditional Middle Eastern and Turkish dessert made from kataifi — fine shredded wheat pastry dough — layered with or filled with soft, mild white cheese (typically akkawi, nabulsi, or mozzarella), baked until golden and crisp, then immediately drenched in fragrant sugar syrup flavoured with rose water or orange blossom water. The result is a dessert with a crispy, buttery exterior, a warm, stretchy molten cheese interior, and a sweet, floral syrup that permeates every layer. Kunafa is typically served hot or warm, often garnished with crushed pistachios. It is a staple celebratory and street-food dessert across the Levant, Turkey, and the broader Arab world, with regional variations in cheese type, pastry form (fine-shred vs. semolina-based), and syrup intensity. Nutritionally, kunafa is energy-dense (~320 kcal/100 g), high in refined carbohydrates and sugar (~43 g and ~22 g per 100 g respectively), and contains moderate saturated fat (~7.8 g/100 g) from butter and cheese. Its medium glycemic index (~65) combined with a large serving size produces a high glycemic load, making it a food of concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or weight. It provides modest amounts of calcium and protein from the cheese component.
common_user_queries: What cheese is used in kunafa?
What is the GI of kunafa?
What is the difference between kunafa and baklava?
Where can I get kunafa in Singapore?

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB, ICMR-NIN, USDA FoodData Central) contains a direct entry for kunafa as a prepared dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central component entries (shredded wheat pastry, mozzarella cheese, butter, sugar syrup). GI is estimated by analogy with syrup-soaked wheat pastry desserts from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported for this composite dish in the USDA FoodData Central component entries used for estimation. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the source component data. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the source component data. Vitamin C (vitamin_c_mg) is 0.0; confirmed zero as kunafa contains no fruit or vegetable components and USDA component data reports negligible or zero vitamin C for all constituent ingredients. The food is classified as Turkish cuisine in the input source (field survey data, Sultan Palace restaurant); however, kunafa/knafeh is of broader Levantine/Middle Eastern origin and is widely prepared across the Arab world and Turkey. The Turkish variant 'künefe' typically uses a specific local unsalted cheese (hatay peyniri) and is served on a plate with kaymak (clotted cream); the restaurant version in Singapore may use mozzarella as a substitute. GI source match is analogous_estimate; a direct GI measurement for kunafa was not located in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (65 × 43.0 × 1.50) / 100 = 42.1. Serving size of 150 g is used as a representative restaurant portion based on typical Middle Eastern dessert serving conventions.
